author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
title: Conclusion.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: I have said a great deal, and, perchance, I could long ago have persuaded you what I wished, and am still speaking this to such intelligent minds as if they were obtuse, to whom even what is too much is not enough.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But let them pardon me, for a new question has compelled me to this.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Because, although in my former little treatises I had proved by sufficiently appropriate proofs that faith also was the gift of God, there was found this ground of contradiction, viz., that those testimonies were good for this purpose, to show that the increase of faith was God’s gift, but that the beginning of faith, whereby a man first of all believes in Christ, is of the man himself, and is not the gift of God,—but that God requires this, so that when it has preceded, other gifts may follow, as it were on the ground of this merit, and these are the gifts of God; and that none of them is given freely, although in them God’s grace is declared, which is not grace except as being gratuitous.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And you see how absurd all this is.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Wherefore I determined, as far as I could, to set forth that this very beginning also is God’s gift.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And if I have done this at a greater length than perhaps those on whose account I did it might wish, I am prepared to be reproached for it by them, so long as they nevertheless confess that, although at greater length than they wished, although with the disgust and weariness of those that understand, I have done what I have done: that is, I have taught that even the beginning of faith, as continence, patience, righteousness, piety, and the rest, concerning which there is no dispute with them, is God’s gift.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Let this, therefore, be the end of this treatise, lest too great length in this one may give offence.
